titleStmt.title: Letter from M.B. McDonough to Mabel FitzGerald, 16 June 1916.
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: M. B. McDonough

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter is from M.B. MacDonough to Mabel FitzGerald (née Washington McConnell) (1884-1958), wife of politician and intellectual Desmond FitzGerald (1888-1947). The writer's husband is being transferred from Wandsworth Prison to Wales and she acknowledges a letter from her husband which Mrs. FitzGerald had relayed to her. She sympathises with Mrs FitzGerald on her husband's 10 year sentence and hopes for a free Ireland soon when all can be reunited with their loved ones.Mabel Fitzgerald was the wife of Irish Volunteer Desmond Fitzgerald. Mabel was a former governess in London. She later vigorously canvassed her former acquaintances in London's liberal circles for support for Francis Sheehy Skeffington during his hunger strike in 1915. Both Mabel and Desmond would serve in the GPO garrison during Easter Week.

correspAction.placeName: 2 Loretto Villas, Bray, County Wicklow, Ireland.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
High St Bray June 16 1916 Dear Mrs. Fitzgerald I wish to return
you very many thanks
for your kindness in
sending me my Husband's
letter of whome I have
not seen since he was
sent to Wansworth & is
now sent to Wales not knowing
when he may come home
I suppose they will keep
him as long as they like.
I expect that he may be
home soon. I also wish
to sympathise with you
or yours getting ten years
imprisonment an awful
sentence for a brave & beautiful cause. Cheer up as Mr. Fitzgerald
may not be ten moments
away untill Ireland is
a free Nation. Hoping it
will be & then we will have our dear uns again. Thanking you most
sincerely Yours M B McDonough
